title: How to Use Intuition When Making Lifestyle Changes
meta_description: Feeling stuck between fear and intuition? Learn to read your body's physical signals for real guidance. Discover how to tell the difference when making big lifestyle changes.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Making Lifestyle Changes
og_description: Feeling stuck between fear and intuition? Learn to read your body's physical signals for real guidance. Discover how to tell the difference when making big lifestyle changes.
body:
You Feel Stuck, And You're Not Sure If It's Fear Or Your Gut

You're standing at a crossroads. Maybe it's a career change that's been whispering to you for months. Maybe it's finally leaving a relationship that's been draining you dry. Maybe it's a move to a new city, a new diet, a new way of living that feels both terrifying and necessary. And you're asking yourself: "Is this my intuition, or is this just my anxiety being loud?"

I hear this question every single day. And here's the honest truth: you can't always tell the difference in the moment. Not at first. Not until you learn the language your body is speaking. Because intuition isn't a voice in your head - it's a physical signal. It's a gut feeling, a chest tightening, a sudden knowing that doesn't come from logic. And when you're making big lifestyle changes, that signal gets mixed up with all the noise of fear, hope, and old patterns.

So let's get practical. Let's talk about how to actually use your intuition without losing your mind. Because you don't need to be a psychic medium to do this. You just need to learn how to listen to your own nervous system. Right?!

"Your intuition isn't a magic answer machine. It's a compass that points toward your deepest alignment - but you still have to walk the path yourself."

Why Your Intuition Gets Garbled During Big Changes

When you're making a lifestyle change - whether it's quitting a job, ending a friendship, or starting a new health routine - your nervous system goes into overdrive. It's not just the change itself. It's the unknown. And your brain hates the unknown. Your brain is wired for survival, not for expansion. So when you try to listen to your intuition, your brain jumps in with all its protective mechanisms: fear, doubt, overthinking, and old stories about what's "safe."

Here's what happens. You feel a pull toward something new. Maybe it's a quiet nudge to start a morning meditation practice. Maybe it's a strong urge to leave a job that's been making you sick. But as soon as you feel that pull, your mind starts spinning: "But what if I fail? What if I'm wrong? What if everyone thinks I'm crazy?" And suddenly, you can't tell if that initial pull was intuition or just wishful thinking.

That's normal. That's human. And it's not a sign that your intuition is broken. It's a sign that you need to learn how to separate the signal from the static.

The First Step: Get Out Of Your Head And Into Your Body

Intuition doesn't live in your brain. It lives in your body. So if you're trying to figure out whether a decision is right for you by thinking about it, you're already on the wrong track. The thinking mind is great for logistics - budgets, timelines, pros and cons lists. But it's terrible at knowing what's actually aligned with your soul.

So here's what I want you to try. Next time you're facing a big decision, stop. Take three slow breaths. Then bring your attention to your body. Not your thoughts. Your body. Notice where you feel tension. Notice where you feel ease. Notice any sensations - a flutter in your stomach, a tightness in your chest, a warmth in your hands.

Now, imagine the choice you're considering. Let's say it's leaving your current job. As you picture yourself walking out that door for the last time, what happens in your body? Does your chest open up? Does your stomach relax? Or does everything tighten up like you're bracing for impact?

That physical response is your intuition speaking. Not the voice in your head that says "but the economy is bad." Not the voice that says "but you've been here for ten years." Just the raw, physical response of your nervous system. And that response will tell you more than any pros and cons list ever could.

Does that land? I hope so, because this is the foundation of everything else I'm going to share with you.

How To Tell The Difference Between Intuition And Fear

This is the million-dollar question, isn't it? And the answer is simpler than you think. Intuition feels neutral or expansive. Fear feels contractive and urgent.

Let me explain. When your intuition is guiding you toward something, the feeling is usually calm, clear, and steady. It doesn't shout. It doesn't panic. It just knows. Even if the decision is scary - like leaving a marriage or starting a business - the intuitive pull has a quality of quiet certainty underneath the fear. It feels like a deep breath.

Fear, on the other hand, feels like a trap. It's tight, fast, and reactive. Fear says "you have to decide RIGHT NOW." It spins stories about worst-case scenarios. It makes your heart race and your palms sweat. Fear is the voice that says "if you don't do this perfectly, you'll fail forever."

So here's a practical test. When you're considering a lifestyle change, ask yourself: "Does this decision feel like a 'yes' in my body, or does it feel like a 'no' that I'm trying to override?" If it feels like a yes - even a quiet, hesitant yes - that's intuition. If it feels like a no that you're trying to convince yourself out of, that's fear.

And if you're still confused? Give it time. Real intuition doesn't disappear after three days of sitting with it. Fear fades when you stop feeding it with attention. Intuition stays steady.

Using Intuition To work through Lifestyle Changes: A Step-By-Step Process

Okay, let's get concrete. Here's a four-step process you can use anytime you're facing a big change. I've been teaching this for years, and it works because it honors both your intuition and your rational mind.

Step 1: Gather Information Without Overwhelm

Before you can listen to your intuition, you need to know what you're dealing with. So do your research. If you're thinking about moving to a new city, learn about the cost of living, job market, and neighborhoods. If you're considering a new diet, read about the science behind it. But set a boundary: give yourself a specific amount of time - say, one week - to gather information, and then stop. Don't keep researching forever. That's just fear in disguise.

One book that helped me understand how to trust my body's signals during this phase is The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It's not a "psychic" book, but it explains how your nervous system holds memory and truth in ways your conscious mind can't access. See on Amazon (paid link)

Step 2: Create Space For Stillness

You can't hear intuition when you're running on fumes. You need quiet. Not necessarily meditation - just a few minutes where you're not consuming information, scrolling your phone, or talking. Go for a walk without headphones. Sit with a cup of tea and stare out the window. Let your mind settle. In that stillness, your intuition has room to surface.

Step 3: Check In With Your Body

Remember the body check I described earlier? Do that. But do it more than once. Do it in the morning, when you're fresh. Do it after you've eaten. Do it when you're tired. Notice if the physical sensation changes. Intuition is consistent. Fear is reactive to circumstances.

Step 4: Make A Small, Reversible Test

This is the most underrated step. You don't have to make a huge leap of faith. You can test the waters. If you're thinking about changing careers, take a single class in that field. If you're considering ending a relationship, spend a weekend apart and see how you feel. If you're drawn to a new spiritual practice, try it for ten minutes a day for a week. Small tests give your intuition real-world data to work with. And they reduce the fear of "what if I'm wrong?"

For more on how to trust yourself through small experiments, I love The Power of Now by Eckhart Tolle. It's a classic for a reason. See on Amazon (paid link)

What To Do When Your Intuition Says "Yes" But Your Life Says "Not Yet"

This is a tough one. You feel a strong intuitive pull toward a change - say, quitting your job to travel the world - but your practical life isn't ready. You have bills. You have a lease. You have people depending on you. So what do you do?

First, don't force it. Intuition doesn't require reckless action. It requires alignment. And alignment can happen in stages. If your intuition says "yes" to a big change, but the timing isn't right, that doesn't mean your intuition is wrong. It means you need to build a bridge.

Ask your intuition: "What's the next smallest step I can take toward this?" Maybe that means saving money for six months. Maybe it means having a conversation with your partner. Maybe it means starting a side hustle. The intuitive pull is the destination. The practical steps are the path. Both are valid.

And here's something people don't talk about enough: sometimes your intuition is telling you to leave something, not because you're ready to leave, but because you need to start preparing. Honor that. Don't ignore the signal just because you can't act on it today.

How To Handle The Doubt That Comes After You Decide

You make a decision. You feel good about it. And then, three days later, doubt creeps in. You start questioning everything. Did I make the right choice? What if I'm wrong? This is normal. This is so normal. It doesn't mean your intuition was wrong. It means your ego is trying to protect you from uncertainty.

The key here is to remember the quality of the intuitive signal you felt before you decided. Go back to that moment. Recall the physical sensation. The calm. The knowing. That's your anchor. When doubt comes, you don't have to argue with it. You just remind yourself: "I felt a clear 'yes' in my body. I trust that signal. I don't have to feel certain every second."

One of the best books I've read on navigating this kind of spiritual uncertainty is When Things Fall Apart by Pema Chödrön. It's not about making decisions, but it's about staying sane when everything feels shaky. See on Amazon (paid link)

Why Your Intuition Might Be Screaming At You Right Now

If you've been ignoring your intuition for a long time - months, years, even decades - it's going to get loud. It might show up as anxiety, as physical pain, as a constant feeling of restlessness. That's not a punishment. That's your soul trying to get your attention before you waste another year in a life that doesn't fit.

I've seen this so many times. Someone comes to me and says, "I feel like I'm going crazy. I can't sleep. I'm irritable. I keep getting signs everywhere." And when we dig into it, it's because they've been suppressing an intuitive knowing for years. They knew they needed to leave that job. They knew that relationship was over. They knew they weren't living in alignment. But they kept overriding the signal with logic, with fear, with "I'll do it later."

Your intuition doesn't give up. It just gets louder. And eventually, it will force a crisis if you don't listen. That's not a curse. That's love. Your soul loves you enough to disrupt your comfort.

Practical Tools To Strengthen Your Intuition For Daily Life

You don't need to be born psychic to have a strong intuition. You just need to practice. Here are three simple tools you can use every day to strengthen your intuitive muscle:

  • The Two-Minute Body Scan: Before you check your phone in the morning, lie still for two minutes and just feel your body. Notice any sensations without trying to change them. This trains you to notice subtle signals.
  • The "First Thought" Practice: When you're facing a small decision - what to eat for lunch, which route to take - notice the first thought that pops into your head. Not the second one. Not the logical one. The first one. That's often intuitive.
  • The Gratitude Pause: At the end of each day, ask yourself: "What felt aligned today? What didn't?" This helps you recognize the pattern of intuitive signals over time.

If you want a deeper dive into building intuitive trust, I highly recommend The Gift of Fear by Gavin de Becker. It's not a spiritual book, but it's a masterclass in how your body's warning signals work. See on Amazon (paid link)

When To Trust Your Intuition Over Other People's Advice

This is where it gets sticky. Because the people who love you will give you advice. And some of it will be good. But a lot of it will be based on their fears, their unfulfilled dreams, and their ideas of what's safe. So how do you know when to listen to them and when to trust yourself?

Here's my rule: other people's advice is useful for logistics, not for alignment. Your friend can help you think through the financial implications of a career change. Your partner can help you plan the timeline for a move. But no one else can tell you whether a decision feels right in your body. That's your domain.

So when someone gives you advice, notice how it lands in your body. If it makes you feel tight and small, it's probably not aligned. If it makes you feel a sense of relief or expansion - even if it's scary - it might be worth considering. But ultimately, you're the only one who has to live with the consequences. So you're the only one who gets the final say.

The Bright Wound: Why Sensitive People Struggle With This

If you're reading this and thinking, "But I've always been told I'm too sensitive, too emotional, too much," let me name something. That sensitivity you've been shamed for? That's your intuitive gift. It's not a flaw. It's a finely tuned nervous system that picks up on things other people miss. The problem isn't your sensitivity. The problem is that you've been taught to distrust it.

You've been told that your gut feelings are "overreactions." You've been told that your emotional responses are "dramatic." You've been trained to override your own knowing with other people's logic. And that training is what's making it hard to hear your intuition now. You have to unlearn that. You have to reclaim the trust in your own body.

That's what I call "The Bright Wound" - the pain of being sensitive in a world that doesn't understand it, and the brilliance that comes when you finally own it. You're not broken. You're calibrated for a wider range. And that includes the ability to feel truth that others can't access.

A Final Word: You Already Know What To Do

I'm going to tell you something that might be uncomfortable. You already know what the right next step is. You've known it for a while. The reason you're reading this article isn't because you don't know - it's because you're scared to trust what you know. And that's okay. That's human.

But here's the thing: your intuition won't wait forever. It will keep sending signals until you either listen or force it into silence. And forced silence is what leads to burnout, regret, and a life that feels like someone else's. So take a breath. Feel into your body. Ask yourself: "What do I really want?" Not what you should want. Not what's practical. What you actually, deep down, want.

That's your intuition. And it's been waiting for you to trust it.

If you're ready to go deeper into understanding your own intuitive patterns and how they show up in your life, I've created a free assessment that can help you identify where you're blocking your own knowing. Take the free assessment here or check out the intuition style quiz to start untangling the signals from the noise.
